impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense applies when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court rejected Ingevity's argument that its honeycombs were 'nonstaple goods' protected from antitrust liability, because the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., in air-intake systems not covered by the patent). This case reinforces that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of anticompetitive tying can use this precedent to challenge patent holders who condition licenses on exclusive purchasing of unpatented goods with substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with product exclusivity agreements face significant antitrust liability risk if the tied products have substantial uses outside the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls to a model are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages may be based on foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia University, holding that the asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at Alice step one, with remand needed on step two. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. This decision narrows patent eligibility for cybersecurity-related software patents and limits extraterritorial damages recovery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent assertions benefit from the court's reinforcement of Alice's abstract idea doctrine and its rejection of extraterritorial damages theories.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in cybersecurity and software fields face heightened risk that their claims will be invalidated under § 101, and cannot recover damages based on foreign sales.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact preclude summary judgment on both inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (granted in favor of Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) from a predecessor company but allegedly failed to disclose them during patent prosecution. The case highlights the intersection of patent fraud and antitrust liability when a company procures patents through potential inequitable conduct and then asserts them against competitors.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence on factual development before summary disposition. Competitors alleging Walker Process fraud retain the ability to pursue antitrust claims to trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions face heightened scrutiny regarding disclosure obligations during prosecution, with potential antitrust liability for attempted monopolization.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring content delivery to device screen sizes and capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing a $2.5 million jury verdict in GoTV's favor on the '715 patent. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device capabilities and entered judgment for Netflix. This decision significantly impacts content delivery and streaming technology patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies like Netflix gain strong precedent against patent assertions covering basic adaptive content delivery functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content adaptation for different devices face serious eligibility challenges under § 101.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the presumption against extraterritoriality bars infringement claims where the accused multicore processor selection method is performed partly outside the United States, and whether doctrine of equivalents theories of infringement were properly rejected.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement of VLSI's '836 patent directed to selecting cores in multicore processors. The court found genuine issues of material fact regarding extraterritoriality and reversed on that ground, while affirming the rejection of VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory. The case provides important guidance on how extraterritoriality analysis applies to semiconductor design and manufacturing processes that span multiple jurisdictions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting semiconductor and processor design patents gain clarity that extraterritoriality does not automatically bar claims where significant infringing conduct occurs domestically.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies with global chip design operations face continued exposure to U.S. patent claims even when some steps occur overseas.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after having missed the statutory deadline for seeking a stay of the original action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action by dismissing and refiling its own case to reset the 30-day clock under § 1659(a)(2). The court found this would allow gamesmanship to circumvent the statutory time limits Congress established for coordinating ITC and district court proceedings. This ruling is significant for pharmaceutical and other companies navigating parallel ITC investigations and district court patent litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing ITC investigations benefit from certainty that opponents cannot manipulate stay provisions through strategic refiling of district court actions.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the statutory stay deadline cannot cure the deficiency by refiling actions, limiting their ability to delay parallel district court proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated § 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents on wearable blood oxygenation measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing watches. The court found no error in the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This high-profile decision has major implications for the consumer electronics and health technology industries.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with innovative health technology gain confidence that ITC exclusion orders can effectively block importation of infringing consumer electronics products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer electronics companies incorporating health-monitoring features face significant import exclusion risk if those features are covered by third-party patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify beyond personal knowledge at summary judgment regarding prior art sales, and whether source code constitutes hearsay for purposes of proving the on-sale bar under pre-AIA 35 U.S.C. § 102(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the on-sale bar, holding that a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative's testimony about historical product sales was admissible even though the witness lacked personal knowledge, and that computer source code is not hearsay because it constitutes machine instructions rather than human assertions. This decision clarifies important evidentiary standards for proving prior art sales and the admissibility of software code in patent invalidity proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from broader admissibility of corporate deposition testimony and source code evidence when proving prior art sales for invalidity defenses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face expanded evidentiary tools available to challengers, as corporate representatives can testify about historical sales without personal knowledge and source code is admissible as non-hearsay.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a district court erred in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft involving Azure cloud networking technology. The court held that the district court improperly excluded the damages evidence and vacated the resulting summary judgment. This decision is significant for patent damages methodology and the standards for excluding expert testimony in technology patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from greater protection against overly aggressive Daubert exclusion of damages experts, ensuring cases proceed to trial on the merits.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants like Microsoft face reduced ability to dispose of patent cases at summary judgment through challenges to damages expert methodology.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to operating the PACER system.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees in violation of the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representative nonprofits, finding that hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed. This decision has broad implications for legal industry participants who use the PACER system.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that paid PACER fees during the class period will receive reimbursement, and the settlement constrains future fee-setting by the judiciary.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether restaurants that applied for but did not receive Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants have Tucker Act jurisdiction to seek damages where the SBA allegedly failed to award grants in the statutory order of receipt.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses had Tucker Act jurisdiction to pursue claims for unpaid RRF grants, holding that the statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in order of receipt) created money-mandating obligations enforceable against the United States. The court found that the SBA's failure to award grants in the order applications were received stated a viable claim. This ruling opens the door for potentially significant damages claims by businesses that were passed over for COVID-19 relief grants.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied federal grants under mandatory statutory frameworks gain a viable path to seek damages when agencies fail to follow statutory distribution requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for grant distribution errors under mandatory spending statutes, which could affect administration of future relief programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a, and whether that statute abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the governing statute and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to align its confidentiality practices with statutory requirements. This decision may significantly increase transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies involved in trade disputes and the public gain greater access to information in trade remedy proceedings, promoting transparency and enabling better-informed challenges to duty determinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit commercially sensitive information in ITC questionnaire responses face reduced confidentiality protections, potentially exposing proprietary business data.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress gave the Director unreviewable discretion over institution decisions and that the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's flexibility in managing its IPR docket but limits challengers' ability to contest discretionary denial policies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's validation of the PTO's flexibility in declining to institute IPRs, providing greater stability for issued patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners face continued risk that the PTO may deny institution based on discretionary policies issued without public input or formal rulemaking.

impacts.caseName: Life Science Logistics, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a government agency's decision to override the automatic CICA stay of contract performance during a GAO bid protest was arbitrary and capricious.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that GSA's override of the automatic CICA stay during LSL's bid protest of a Strategic National Stockpile contract award was arbitrary and capricious. The court applied the capable-of-repetition-yet-evading-review mootness exception to reach the merits, finding the government's Determination and Findings insufficient to justify the override. This decision reinforces protections for incumbent contractors during the bid protest process and limits agencies' ability to override statutory stays.
impacts.positiveImplications: Government contractors benefit from stronger protections during bid protests, as agencies face heightened scrutiny when overriding automatic performance stays.
impacts.negativeImplications: Government agencies face greater constraints on their ability to proceed with contract performance during bid protests, potentially causing operational delays in critical programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in patents covering adaptive bitrate streaming of partially encrypted media content.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming partially encrypted media content, vacating the Board's finding that Netflix failed to prove obviousness. The court adopted a broader construction of 'encryption information' and remanded for further proceedings. This decision impacts the streaming technology sector by potentially invalidating key patent claims covering encrypted content delivery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies like Netflix benefit from broader claim constructions that may render competitors' encryption-related patents obvious over prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming and digital rights management space face risk that key claims may be invalidated on remand under the broader claim construction.
